title: TTA Voting and Aggregation
concept: Once the model predicts on all augmented test images, we must aggregate their confidence scores. Calculating the average probability distribution (mean ensemble) is the standard way to determine the final, robust prediction.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is the main danger of using class-altering transforms (like vertical flips on traffic lights) during TTA?

options.text: They create unrealistic or incorrect inputs, degrading the final averaged prediction accuracy
They make the model parameters twice as large
They cause a compilation error in the GPU code

exercises.explain: Yes! Class-altering transforms degrade the averaged accuracy by feeding incorrect inputs.

cards.title: Averaging the Votes
cards.body: You've generated 4 augmented versions of your test image and run them through the CNN. The model outputs a probability distribution for each: one says 90% cat, another says 80% cat, one says 40% cat (due to a bad crop), and the last says 70%. How do we combine these votes?

cards.title: TTA Aggregation (Mean Ensemble)
cards.body: TTA aggregation calculates the mean probability distribution across all augmented passes. If we run $N$ passes, we sum the probability vectors for each class and divide by $N$. The class with the highest average score wins.

cards.title: The student test average
cards.body: If you take a test 4 times under slightly different conditions, your final grade isn't just your worst score (40%) or your best score (90%). It's the average of all 4 attempts. Averaging smooths out the impact of one unlucky crop or bad angle.

cards.title: A Worked Voting Example
cards.body:
Imagine 3 TTA passes predicting [Cat, Dog] probabilities:
- Pass 1: `[0.9, 0.1]` (original)
- Pass 2: `[0.8, 0.2]` (flipped)
- Pass 3: `[0.4, 0.6]` (bad crop)
- **Average**: `[(0.9+0.8+0.4)/3, (0.1+0.2+0.6)/3]` = `[0.7, 0.3]`. The final winner is **Cat** (70%).

cards.title: Don't just average the hard labels
cards.body: It's a myth that you should average the final predicted names (hard votes like 'Cat', 'Cat', 'Dog' -> 'Cat'). You must average the **raw probabilities (soft votes)**. Soft voting preserves the model's uncertainty, giving much more weight to high-confidence correct guesses.

cards.title: Hard vs. Soft Voting in TTA
cards.body: How the two voting aggregation methods compare:

left.text:
Averages final class labels
Ignores confidence values
Less sensitive to model nuance

right.text:
Averages raw probability vectors
Preserves confidence values
Standard, robust default

exercises.prompt: How are predictions combined in standard Test-Time Augmentation?

options.text: By calculating the average (mean) probability distribution across all augmented passes
By picking only the pass with the lowest confidence score
By multiplying the probability vectors together
By letting the GPU pick a random pass to keep

exercises.explain: Correct! Soft voting (averaging probability distributions) is the standard aggregation method.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Soft voting in TTA is generally preferred over hard voting because it preserves the model's confidence nuance.
exercises.explain: True. Soft voting keeps confidence metrics intact, resulting in more reliable ensembles.

exercises.sentence: Instead of hard labels, TTA aggregates raw ___ vectors to preserve confidence values.
exercises.explain: Averaging probability vectors (soft voting) makes the final prediction more robust.

exercises.prompt: If TTA Pass A predicts [0.9 Cat, 0.1 Dog] and Pass B predicts [0.7 Cat, 0.3 Dog], what is the aggregated TTA probability?
exercises.explain: Correct! The mean is (0.9+0.7)/2 = 0.8 Cat and (0.1+0.3)/2 = 0.2 Dog.

exercises.prompt: Why is soft voting mathematically superior to hard voting when aggregating TTA passes?

options.text: It allows a high-confidence correct guess (e.g. 99% Cat) to override a low-confidence wrong guess (e.g. 51% Dog), preserving true signal
It cuts the latency of the forward pass in half
It guarantees that the average is always exactly 1.0
It is simpler to write in basic HTML code

exercises.explain: Exactly! Soft voting lets high-confidence signal dominate noise. Next: when (and when not) to use TTA in production!
